lessons.title: Grammar Basics: Subject-Verb Agreement
lessons.description: Learn how subjects and verbs must agree in number (singular or plural) for clear and correct English sentences.

options: Every one of the students want to pass the exam.
Every one of the students wants to pass the exam.
Every one of the students are wanting to pass the exam.
Every one of the students have wanted to pass the exam.

questions.text: Neither the teacher nor the students ______ present at the meeting.
The bouquet of red roses ______ a beautiful fragrance to the room.
Economics ______ considered a challenging subject by many students.
A number of tourists ______ visiting the ancient castle today.

lessons.title: Daily Vocabulary: Idioms & Expressions
lessons.description: Expand your vocabulary with common idiomatic expressions used in daily conversations.

questions.text: What does the idiom 'bite the bullet' mean?
options: To make a sudden decision to travel
To face a difficult situation with courage
To speak in a very loud voice

questions.text: If someone tells you to 'break a leg', what are they wishing you?
What does it mean to say a decision is 'up in the air'?
options: It is still uncertain or undecided
It is very simple to make

questions.text: What does the idiom 'under the weather' mean?

lessons.title: Everyday Conversations: Polite Phrases
lessons.description: Practice expressing opinions, agreeing, disagreeing, and making polite requests.

questions.text: Which phrase is the most polite way to disagree in a discussion?
options: You are completely wrong about this.
I see your point, but I have a slightly different view.
That makes no sense at all.
I don't want to listen to your opinion.

questions.text: How would you politely request someone to speak louder?
options: Could you please speak a bit louder? I can't hear you clearly.
I don't understand your quiet voice.

questions.text: What is the best way to politely accept an invitation?
options: I will think about it later.
Sure, I've got nothing better to do.
Thank you so much! I would love to attend.

questions.text: How do you ask for clarification when you don't understand an assignment?
options: What do you want me to do?
Could you please explain this part again? I want to make sure I understand it.
This task is too hard and unclear.
